title: Agent Fundamentals
description: Tool calling, planning, reflection, memory, agent loops, safety, and human-in-the-loop patterns.

questions.title: What is the difference between an agent and a workflow?
questions.scenario: An enterprise automation team runs agent that searches knowledge, calls APIs, creates tickets, and drafts actions. A recent review found that the agent acts autonomously without enough control over tools, memory, and termination; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for difference between an agent and a workflow measured by task success, tool-call precision, step count, intervention rate, and cost per task.
questions.expectedAnswer: difference between an agent and a workflow requires comparing at least two viable choices instead of assuming one technique is universally better. In agent that searches knowledge, calls APIs, creates tickets, and drafts actions, the practical approach is bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from tool schemas, action logs, memory records, approvals, and task outcomes, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use task success, tool-call precision, step count, intervention rate, and cost per task, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption. difference between an agent and a workflow addresses that risk through bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using task success, tool-call precision, step count, intervention rate, and cost per task.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from tool schemas, action logs, memory records, approvals, and task outcomes. Implement the change with LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in agent that searches knowledge, calls APIs, creates tickets, and drafts actions, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor task success, tool-call precision, step count, intervention rate, and cost per task so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: How does tool calling work in an agent?

commonMistakes: Explaining difference between an agent and a workflow as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of task success, tool-call precision, step count, intervention rate, and cost per task.
Ignoring the main risk: unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that difference between an agent and a workflow improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying difference between an agent and a workflow, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: An enterprise automation team applied difference between an agent and a workflow in agent that searches knowledge, calls APIs, creates tickets, and drafts actions. The baseline suffered from unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation using LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, then evaluated the change against task success, tool-call precision, step count, intervention rate, and cost per task. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for difference between an agent and a workflow, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.scenario: An enterprise automation team runs agent that searches knowledge, calls APIs, creates tickets, and drafts actions. questions.expectedAnswer: agent fallback strategy belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from tool schemas, action logs, memory records, approvals, and task outcomes, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures.
